slides.title: Decision Theory and Classifiers:
slides.content:
✓ Bayesian Decision Theory.
✓ The Classification Problem.
✓ Bayesian Classifiers for Normal Distribution.
✓ Maximum A Posteriori (MAP).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: What is Decision Theory?
slides.content:
A set of quantitative methods for reaching optimal decisions.
Is concerned with the reasoning underlying an agent’s choices,
whether this is a mundane choice between taking the bus or
getting a taxi, or a more far-reaching choice about whether to
pursue a demanding political career.
Layman term: how we can decide what action should be taken
that could end up with the best result?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: Bayes’ Rule
slides.content:
“Bayesian”: refer to inference methods that represent “degrees of
certainty” using probability theory, and which leverage Bayes’ rule, to
update the degree of certainty given data.
Layman term: Update our belief based on prior belief and data so that we infer
something with a certain degree of uncertainty.
Bayes’ rule: a formula for computing the probability distribution over
possible values of an unknown quantity 𝐻 given observed data 𝑌 = 𝑦.
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦|𝐻 = ℎ)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 =
𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦)
Follows the product rule of probability: 𝑝 ℎ 𝑦 𝑝 𝑦 = 𝑝 ℎ 𝑝 𝑦 ℎ = 𝑝(ℎ, 𝑦)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 : posterior distribution / conditional distribution.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: Bayes’ Rule: in Detail
slides.content:
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦|𝐻 = ℎ)
𝑝 𝐻 = ℎ 𝑌 = 𝑦 =
𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦)
𝑝(𝐻): what we know about possible values of 𝐻 before we see any data –
the prior distribution (layman terms: prior belief).
𝑝(𝑌|𝐻 = ℎ): the distribution over possible outcomes 𝑌 we expect to see if
𝐻 = ℎ – the likelihood (layman terms: the observed data).
Multiplying the prior distribution and the likelihood C unnormalized joint
distribution 𝑝(𝐻 = ℎ, 𝑌 = 𝑦). Convert into a normalized distribution by
dividing by 𝑝(𝑌 = 𝑦), which is denoted as the marginal likelihood.
𝑝(𝐻 = ℎ|𝑌 = 𝑦): represents our new belief state (updated belief) about
the possible values of 𝐻 – the posterior distribution.
posterior ∝ prior × likelihood
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: (Bayesian) Decision Theory: in Detail
slides.content:
In decision theory, we assume the decision maker, or agent, has a set of
possible actions, 𝒜, to choose from.
Example: doctor treat someone who may have cancer, actions 𝑎 ∈ 𝒜:
Do nothing;
Give patient expensive drugs with bad side effect but can save their life;
We can compute (implicitly) the cost of treating a person (test = 0: cancer
negative; age = 0: young age; action = 1: apply the drugs).
cancer
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

cancer
Every action 𝑎 ∈ 𝒜 has cost and benefits, depending on the underlying
state of nature ℎ ∈ ℋ. How can we explicitly model such cost / benefit?
Define: loss function ℓ(ℎ, 𝑎) – loss incurred if action 𝑎 is taken at state ℎ.
The next question: how to leverage this loss?
Equivalently: how can we actually know quantitatively what is the best action if we
compute the loss function?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Define: loss function ℓ(ℎ, 𝑎) – action 𝑎 taken at state ℎ.
Compute a posterior expected loss (risk) for each possible 𝑎: (where the
data is defined as 𝑥)
𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 ≜ 𝔼 ℓ ℎ, 𝑎 = ෍ ℓ ℎ, 𝑎 𝑝(ℎ|𝑥)
ℎ 𝑥
𝑝
ℎ∈ℋ
∗
We thus define the optimal policy 𝜋 𝑥 , which is also called the Bayes
∗
estimator or Bayes decision rule 𝛿 (𝑥):
∗
𝜋 𝑥 = arg min 𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 = arg min 𝔼 ℓ ℎ, 𝑎
ℎ 𝑥
𝑝
𝑎∈𝒜 𝑎∈𝒜
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:

The optimal policy 𝜋 𝑥 , or Bayes decision rule 𝛿 (𝑥):

If we define a utility function 𝑈(ℎ, 𝑎) as the desirability of each action at
each state, then: 𝑈 ℎ, 𝑎 = −ℓ ℎ, 𝑎

This is defined as the maximum expected utility principle.
Next, we use Bayesian decision theory to decide the optimal class label to
predict given an observed input 𝑥 ∈ 𝒳.

slides.title: Classification with Zero-One Loss
slides.content:
Set the following assumptions:
The states of nature correspond to class labels: ℋ = 𝒴 = {1, … , 𝐶};
The actions correspond to class labels: 𝒜 = 𝒴.
Under the above assumptions, a common loss function (and a
∗
very simple one) is the zero-one loss ℓ ℎ, 𝑎 = ℓ (𝑦 , 𝑦ො):
01
∗ ∗
Or mathematically ℓ 𝑦 , 𝑦ො = 𝕀(𝑦 = 𝑦ො)
01

The posterior expected loss 𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 ≜ 𝔼 ℓ ℎ, 𝑎 :
ℎ 𝑥

∗ ∗
𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 = 𝜌 𝑦ො 𝑥 = 𝑝 𝑦ො ≠ 𝑦 𝑥 = 1 − 𝑝 𝑦ො = 𝑦 𝑥
The action that minimizes the expected loss is to choose the
most probable action:
𝜋 𝑥 = arg max 𝑝(𝑦ො|𝑥)
𝑦ො∈𝒴
This corresponds to the mode of the posterior distribution, also
known as the maximum a posteriori or MAP estimate.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Classification with Reject Option
slides.content:
We may be able to say “I don’t know” instead of returning an
answer that we don’t really trust – choosing the reject option.
Important in domains such as medicine where we may be risk adverse.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P18EdAKuC1U
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

The states of nature correspond to class labels: ℋ = 𝒴 = {1, … , 𝐶};
The actions correspond to class labels: 𝒜 = 𝒴 ∪ {0}.
The loss function is defined as: 0, if 𝑦∗ = 𝑎 and 𝑎 ∈ {1, …, 𝐶}

thresholding the probability – hard threshold!
Instead of picking a single threshold, consider using a set of different thresholds,
and comparing the resulting performance.
For a threshold 𝜏, the decision rule: 𝑦ො 𝑥 = 𝕀(𝑝 𝑦 = 1 𝑥 > 1 − 𝜏).

slides.title: Plotting the ROC Curve: Example
slides.content:
ROC curve – Plot the TPR vs FPR as an implicit function of 𝜏, i.e., plot
TPR vs FPR with varying 𝜏.
How do we compare different ROC curves?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The ROC Curve
slides.content:
Quality of a ROC curve is often summarized using the Area
Under the Curve (AUC). Higher AUC scores better; max is 1.
Another statistic is the Equal Error
Rate (EER), or the cross-over rate
Defined as the value which satisfies
𝐹𝑃𝑅 = 𝐹𝑁𝑅 = 1 − 𝑇𝑃𝑅.
Compute the EER by drawing a line from
the top left to the bottom right and seeing
where it intersects the ROC curve.
Lower EER scores are better; the
minimum is obviously 0 (top left corner).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Class Imbalance and the ROC Curve
slides.content:
In some problems, there is severe class imbalance.
E.g., in information retrieval, the set of negatives (irrelevant items) is
usually much larger than the set of positives (relevant items).
Discuss: would the ROC curve be affected by class imbalance?
Discuss: how would class imbalance affect the effectiveness of ROC?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Precision-Recall Curve
slides.content:
Problems of the ROC curve:
Require well-defined “true positives” and “true negatives”;
However, in some problems, “negative” may not be well-defined.
E.g., detecting objects in images: if the detector works by classifying
patches, then the # of patches examined — the # of true negatives —
is a param of the algorithm, instead of the problem definition.
Can we evaluate the classification computed just from positives?
Precision and the precision-recall curve:
Precision: replace the FPR with a quantity that is computed just from
positives.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The Precision-Recall (PR) Curve
slides.content:
Precision: fraction of our detections are actually positive:
𝒫 𝜏 ≜ 𝑝 𝑦 = 1 𝑦ො = 1, 𝜏 = 𝑇𝑃 /(𝑇𝑃 + 𝐹𝑃 )
𝜏 𝜏 𝜏
Recall (TPR): fraction of the positives we actually detected:
ℛ 𝜏 ≜ 𝑝 𝑦ො = 1 𝑦 = 1, 𝜏 = 𝑇𝑃 /(𝑇𝑃 + 𝐹𝑁 )
𝜏 𝜏 𝜏
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: An Example of PR Curve in Object Detection
slides.content:
Run object detector on all test images (with NMS).
For each category, compute Average Precision
(AP) = area under Precision vs Recall Curve.
For each detection (highest score to lowest score):
If it matches some GT box with loU > 0.5, mark it as
positive and eliminate the GT.
Otherwise mark it as negative.
Plot a point on PR Curve.
Average Precision (AP) = area under PR curve
Mean Average Precision (mAP) = average of AP
for each category.
For "COCO mAP": Compute mAP@thresh for
each loU threshold (0.5, 0.55, 0.6, ..., 0.95) and
take average.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Plotting the PR Curve: Example
slides.content:
PR curve – Plot the precision vs recall as an implicit function of 𝜏, i.e., plot
precision vs recall with varying 𝜏.
How do we compare different PR curves?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The PR Curve
slides.content:
The statistics to summarize the PR curve:
Precision at 𝐾 score: the precision for a fixed recall level;
AUC: the area under the PR curve;
Interpolated precision:
Possible precision does not drop monotonically with
recall. E.g., a classifier has 90% precision at 10% recall,
and 96% precision at 20% recall.
Measure the maximum precision we can achieve with at
least a recall of 10% (which would be 96%).
Average precision: avg of interpolated precisions
Equal to the area under the interpolated PR curve.
The mean average precision (mAP): the mean of the AP
over a set of different PR curves.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: The PR Curve and F-scores
slides.content:
For a given point on the PR curve, can combine
the precision value 𝒫 and the recall value ℛ into a
single statistics:
2
1 1 1 𝛽 1
= +
𝐹 1 + 𝛽2 𝒫 1 + 𝛽2 ℛ
𝛽
If we set 𝛽 = 1, then we get the harmonic mean of
the precision and recall, known as the 𝐹 score.
1
1 1 1 1
= +
𝐹 2 𝒫 𝑅
1
Questions:
Why do we use the “harmonic mean” instead of the
“arithmetic mean” (𝒫 + ℛ)/2?
Would the PR curve be affected by class imbalance?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Probabilistic Prediction Problem
slides.content:
In previous slides: assumed the set of possible actions was to pick a
single class label (or possibly the “reject” action).
More often: the set of possible actions is to pick a probability
distribution over some value of interest:
Perform probabilistic prediction, rather than predicting a specific value.
For Classification: states of nature → class labels: ℋ = 𝒴 = {1, … , 𝐶};
actions → class labels: 𝒜 = 𝒴.
For Probabilistic prediction: states of nature → ℎ = 𝑝(𝑌|𝑥); action →
𝑎 = 𝑞 𝑌 𝑥 .
What we want: to pick 𝑞 to minimize𝔼 ℓ ℎ, 𝑎 = 𝔼[ℓ(𝑝, 𝑞)] for a given 𝑥.
The key: how to define the loss function ℓ(𝑝, 𝑞)?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Decision Theory and Classifiers:
slides.content:
✓ KL-Divergence
✓ Entropy and Mutual Information
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: KL Divergence (KL-Div)
slides.content:
Loss function is measured between two “distributions”.
Kullback Leibler divergence (KL divergence) is a measure of how
one probability distribution is different from a second:
∗
𝑝 𝑦
∗
𝔻 𝑝 ∥ 𝑞 ≜ ෍ 𝑝 𝑦 log
𝐾𝐿
𝑞 𝑦ො
∗
𝑦 ∈𝒴
Here 𝑝 is the true distribution and 𝑞 is the predicted distribution.
KL divergence can be further expanded as:
∗ ∗ ∗
𝔻 𝑝 ∥ 𝑞 = σ 𝑝 𝑦 log 𝑝(𝑦 ) − σ 𝑝 𝑦 log 𝑞 𝑦ො = −ℍ 𝑝 +
𝐾𝐿
ℍ (𝑝, 𝑞). ℍ(𝑝): entropy of 𝑝; ℍ : cross entropy between 𝑝 and 𝑞.
𝑐𝑒 𝑐𝑒
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Entropy
slides.content:
The key component of KL Divergence: entropy.
The entropy of a probability distribution can be interpreted as a
measure of uncertainty, or lack of predictability, associated with a
random variable drawn from a given distribution.
Entropy: define the information content of a data source.
Suppose we observe a sequence of symbols 𝑋 ~ 𝑝 generated from
𝑛
distribution 𝑝. What does it mean by 𝑝 having high / low entropy?
If 𝑝 has high entropy, hard to predict the value of each observation 𝑋 .
𝑛
Hence dataset 𝒟 = 𝑋 , … , 𝑋 has high information content.
1 𝑛
If 𝑝 is a degenerate distribution with 0 entropy, then every 𝑋 will be the
𝑛
same, so 𝒟 does not contain much information.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Entropy for Discrete Random Variables
slides.content:
More specifically, formulate the entropy of a discrete RV 𝑋 with
distribution 𝑝 over 𝐾 states (classes) as:
𝐾
ℍ 𝑋 ≜ − ෍ 𝑝 𝑋 = 𝑘 log 𝑝 𝑋 = 𝑘 = −𝔼 log 𝑝 𝑋
2 𝑋
𝑘
Note that ℍ 𝑋 is equivalent to the notation of ℍ 𝑝 .
Question: when will the distribution obtain the Max / Min Entropy?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

ℍ 𝑋 ≜ − ෍ 𝑝 𝑋 = 𝑘 log 𝑝 𝑋 = 𝑘 = −𝔼 log 𝑝 𝑋
2 𝑋
Discrete distribution with Maximum entropy is uniform distribution.
For a 𝐾-ary RV, the entropy is max-ed if 𝑝(𝑥 = 𝑘) = 1/𝐾; 𝐻 𝑋 = log 𝐾.
2

mass on one state

1) = 𝜃 and 𝑝(𝑋 = 0) = 1 − 𝜃:
ℍ 𝑋 = −[𝑝 𝑋 = 1 log 𝑝 𝑋 = 1 + 𝑝(𝑋 =
2

slides.title: Mutual Information
slides.content:
KL divergence: measure how dissimilar two distributions were.
Then can we measure how dependent two RVs are?
Turn the question of measuring the dependence of two RVs into a
question about the similarity of their distributions.
The notion of Mutual Information (MI) btw two RVs, defined as:
𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 ≜ 𝔻 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 ∥ 𝑝 𝑥 𝑝 𝑦 = ෍ ෍ 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 log
𝐾𝐿
𝑝 𝑥 𝑝(𝑦)
𝑦 𝑥
Note: the notation is defined as 𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 as 𝑋 and/or 𝑌 can represent a
set of variable – 𝕀(𝑋; 𝑌, 𝑍) is the MI between 𝑋 and 𝑌, 𝑍 .
MI is always non-negative: 𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 ≥ 0. Reach the bound of 0 iff
𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 = 𝑝 𝑥 𝑝 𝑦 .

slides.title: Mutual Information: Interpretation
slides.content:
𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 ≜ 𝔻 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 ∥ 𝑝 𝑥 𝑝 𝑦 = ෍ ෍ 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 log
𝐾𝐿
𝑝 𝑥 𝑝(𝑦)
𝑦 𝑥
MI is a KL-Div between the joint and factored marginal distributions:
MI measures the information gain if we update from a model that treats
the two variables as independent 𝑝(𝑥)𝑝(𝑦) to one that models their true
joint density 𝑝(𝑥, 𝑦).
To gain further, re-express MI in terms of joint and conditional entropies:
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋 − ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 = ℍ 𝑌 − ℍ 𝑌|𝑋 , ℍ 𝑌|𝑋 = ℍ 𝑋, 𝑌 − 𝐻(𝑋)
MI between 𝑋 and 𝑌 is thus expressed as the reduction in uncertainty
about 𝑋 after observing 𝑌, or, by symmetry, the reduction in uncertainty
about 𝑌 after observing 𝑋.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 ≜ 𝔻 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 ∥ 𝑝 𝑥 𝑝 𝑦 = ෍ ෍ 𝑝 𝑥, 𝑦 log
𝐾𝐿
𝑝 𝑥 𝑝(𝑦)
𝑦 𝑥
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋 − ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 = ℍ 𝑌 − ℍ 𝑌|𝑋 , ℍ 𝑌|𝑋 = ℍ 𝑋, 𝑌 − 𝐻(𝑋)
An alternative proof that conditioning, on average, reduces entropy!
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋 − ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 ≥ 0 → ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 ≤ ℍ 𝑋
One can also obtain the following equivalent results of MI:
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋, 𝑌 − ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 − ℍ 𝑌|𝑋
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋 + ℍ 𝑌 − ℍ 𝑋, 𝑌
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
Suppose two RVs 𝑋 and 𝑌 are related to the input integer 𝑛 ∈ 1, … , 8 .
Define 𝑋 𝑛 = 1 if 𝑛 is even and 𝑌 𝑛 = 1 if 𝑛 is a prime number. Thus:
1 1 3 3 3 3 1 1

8 8 8 8 8 8 8 8

1 1 3 3 3 3 1 1
ℍ 𝑌 𝑋 ≜ − σ 𝑝 𝑋, 𝑌 log 𝑝 𝑌 𝑋 = − log + log + log + log = 0.81
8 4 8 4 8 4 8 4
Verify that ℍ 𝑌|𝑋 = ℍ 𝑋, 𝑌 − 𝐻 𝑋 = 0.81
𝕀 𝑋; 𝑌 = ℍ 𝑋 − ℍ 𝑋|𝑌 = 1 − 0.81 = 0.19 𝑏𝑖𝑡𝑠

slides.title: Decision Theory and Classifiers:
slides.content:
✓ The Frequentist Decision
✓ Application in the Context of Supervised Learning
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Recall: Frequentist vs. Bayesian
slides.content:
Frequentist interpretation: probabilities represent long run
frequencies of events that can happen multiple times.
E.g., Flipping the coin many times, we expect it to land heads
about half the time.
Bayesian interpretation: quantify uncertainty or ignorance
about something
E.g., The coin is equally likely to land heads/tails on next toss.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Frequentist Decision Theory
slides.content:
In the Frequentist decision theory, treat the unknown state
of nature (denoted by 𝜽 instead of ℎ) as a fixed yet unknown
quantity, and treat the data 𝑥 as random.
Instead of conditioning over 𝑥, we average over it to compute the loss
expect to incur if apply our estimator to different datasets.
Define the frequentist risk of an estimator 𝛿 given an unknown
state of nature 𝜽 as the expected loss when applying 𝛿 to data
𝑥, where the expectation is over the data, sampled from 𝑝(𝑥|𝜽):
𝑅 𝜽, 𝛿 ≜ 𝔼 ℓ 𝜽, 𝛿 𝑥
𝑥 𝜽
𝑝
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
𝑅 𝜽, 𝛿 ≜ 𝔼 ℓ 𝜃, 𝛿 𝑥
𝑥 𝜽
𝑝
In general, the true state of nature 𝜽 that generates the data 𝑥
is unknown, so cannot compute the risk directly. Then how?
One solution: to assume a prior 𝜋 for 𝜃 then average it out –
0
the Bayes Risk (integrated risk):
𝑅 𝛿 ≜ 𝔼 𝑅 𝜽, 𝛿 = න 𝑑𝜽𝑑𝑥𝜋 𝜽 𝑝 𝑥 𝜽 ℓ 𝜽, 𝛿 𝑥
𝜋 𝜋 0

slides.content:
Recall: in the Bayesian decision theory, we define the optimal
policy, also the Bayes estimator or Bayes decision rule as:
∗
𝜋 𝑥 = arg min 𝜌 𝑎 𝑥 = arg min 𝔼 ℓ ℎ, 𝑎
ℎ 𝑥

In the Frequentist decision theory, the optimal estimator is:
𝛿 𝑥 = arg min න 𝑑𝜃𝜋 𝜃 𝑝 𝑥 𝜃 ℓ(𝜃, 𝑎) ≡ arg min න 𝑑𝜃𝑝 𝜃 𝑥 ℓ(𝜃, 𝑎)
0
𝑎 𝑎
The two forms are actually equivalent! What does this mean?
Bayesian approach provides a good way of achieving the frequentist
goal – picking the optimal action case-by-case is optimal on average!
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.content:
Use of a prior might seem undesirable in certain cases. We can
therefore define the maximum risk as:

In supervised learning,
Different unknown state of nature (output 𝑦) for each different input 𝑥,
Estimator 𝛿 is a prediction function 𝑦ො = 𝑓(𝑥),

slides.title: Regularization and Regularized Risk
slides.content:
To avoid the chance of overfitting, it is common to add a
complexity penalty to the objective function – regularization.
With regularization, we obtain the regularized empirical risk:
𝑅 𝑓, 𝒟 = 𝑅 𝑓, 𝐷 + 𝜆𝐶(𝑓)
𝜆
𝐶(𝑓) measures the complexity of the prediction function 𝑓 and 𝜆 ≥ 0 is
the hyperparameter that controls the strength of complexity penalty.
In practice, the regularization is often applied to the model parameters
directly, thus the regularized ER is rewritten: 𝑅 𝜃, 𝒟 = 𝑅 𝜃, 𝐷 + 𝜆𝐶(𝜃).
𝜆
If the loss function is a log loss term, while the regularizer is a negative
1
𝑁
log prior, 𝑅 𝜃, 𝒟 = − σ log 𝑝 𝑦 𝑥 , 𝜃 − 𝜆 log 𝑝 𝜃 . Minimizing this
𝜆 𝑛=1 𝑛 𝑛
𝑁
is exactly equivalent to the MAP estimation.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Occam’s Razor
slides.content:
Occam's razor is the problem-solving principle that recommends
searching for explanations constructed with the smallest possible
set of elements.
Also known as the principle of parsimony or the law of parsimony.
Consider 2 models, a simple one, 𝑚 , and a more complex one, 𝑚 .
1 2
Suppose both can explain the data by suitably optimizing their
෡ ෡
parameters, i.e., 𝑝(𝒟|𝜽 , 𝑚 ) and 𝑝(𝒟|𝜽 , 𝑚 ) are both large.
1 1 2 2
Intuitively should prefer 𝑚 , as it is simpler and as good as 𝑚 .
1 2
In regularization, it corresponds to the complexity penalty, where
more complex models would result in a larger regularization term.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Population Risk Est. with Validation
slides.content:
A simple way to estimate the population risk for a supervised
learning setup: cross-validation.
Partition the dataset into two, one used for training models (training set),
another, the validation set or holdout set, used for assessing the risk.
Note that in supervised learning, the real “test data” are inaccessible.
Process: fit the model on the training set, use its performance
on the validation set as an approx. to the population risk:
Make the dependence of the 𝑅 (𝑓, 𝒟) on the dataset more explicit:
𝜆
1
𝑅 𝜽, 𝒟 = ෍ ℓ 𝑦, 𝑓 𝑥; 𝜽 + 𝜆𝐶(𝜽)
𝜆
𝐷
𝑥,𝑦 ∈𝒟
෡
Also define 𝜽 𝒟 = arg min 𝑅 𝜃, 𝒟 , 𝒟 and 𝒟 are partitions.
𝜆 𝜆 𝑡𝑟 𝑣𝑎𝑙
𝜃

slides.title: Cross-Validation
slides.content:
෡
For each model, we fit it to the training set to obtain 𝜽 𝒟 . Then use the
𝜆 𝑡𝑟
unregularized empirical risk on the validation set as an estimate of the
𝑣𝑎𝑙 ෡
population risk – validation risk: 𝑅 ≜ 𝑅 𝜽 𝒟 , 𝒟 .
𝜆 0 𝜆 𝑡𝑟 𝑣𝑎𝑙
Any shortcomings of this method?
Consider the training data is very limited – not enough data to train on!
Impossible to make reliable estimate of future performance.
Solution?
Cross-validation!
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Split the training data into 𝐾 folds;
For each fold 𝑘 ∈ 1, … , 𝐾 , train on
all but ONE fold, in a round-robin
fashion (in turns, circled around all
possible folds).
Formally, the cross-validation risk is formulated as:
1
𝑐𝑣 𝐾 ෡
𝑅 ≜ σ 𝑅 𝜽 𝒟 , 𝒟

𝒟 is the data in the 𝑘-th fold, and 𝒟 is all the other data. If set 𝐾 = 𝑁,
𝑘 −𝑘
the method is known as leave-one-out cross-validation.
